variations.text: The U.S. federal government expands Medicaid to provide limited coverage restricted to basic primary care and emergency services for all individuals below 150% of the poverty line.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. federal government expands Medicaid to cover essential medical and hospital services, but excluding dental, vision, and mental health benefits, for all individuals below 150% of the poverty line.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. federal government expands Medicaid to cover all individuals below 150% of the poverty line.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. federal government expands Medicaid to cover all individuals below 150% of the poverty line with enhanced benefits including dental, vision, and mental health services.

variations.label: high
variations.text: The U.S. federal government expands Medicaid to cover all individuals below 150% of the poverty line with comprehensive benefits including dental, vision, mental health, prescription drugs, and minimal cost-sharing requirements.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: The U.S. federal government expands Medicaid to cover all individuals below 150% of the poverty line with fully comprehensive benefits including dental, vision, mental health, prescription drugs, long-term care, and zero cost-sharing at point of service.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier increases the comprehensiveness of what Medicaid covers, from emergency-only (tier 1) through standard coverage (tier 4) to fully comprehensive coverage with zero cost-sharing (tier 7). The core scenario—federal Medicaid expansion for individuals below 150% FPL—is preserved throughout. The variation is in the depth and breadth of services covered, not the number of separate programs.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because fully comprehensive coverage with dental, vision, mental health, prescription drugs, long-term care, and zero cost-sharing represents the maximum quality and scope of health coverage, providing the greatest protection against medical need and financial hardship for covered individuals.

variations.label: midpoint

variations.text: Arizona implements well-designed, data-informed water usage regulations with clear enforcement mechanisms and measurable targets to combat drought conditions.

variations.label: most_preferable

original_text: A U.S. federal law decriminalizes all drugs and redirects funding to addiction treatment programs.

variations.label: low

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law decriminalizes all drugs and redirects funding to addiction treatment programs that use some evidence-based approaches but suffer from inconsistent quality, limited aftercare support, and moderate overall recovery outcomes.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law decriminalizes all drugs and redirects funding to addiction treatment programs.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law decriminalizes all drugs and redirects funding to well-structured, evidence-based addiction treatment programs that provide comprehensive care including mental health support and achieve above-average long-term recovery rates.

variations.label: high
variations.text: A U.S. federal law decriminalizes all drugs and redirects funding to rigorously evaluated, best-in-class addiction treatment programs that integrate personalized medical care, mental health services, and sustained community support, yielding high long-term recovery rates.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: A U.S. federal law decriminalizes all drugs and redirects funding to world-leading, comprehensively integrated addiction treatment programs that deliver personalized, cutting-edge medical and psychological care with robust long-term aftercare, achieving the highest documented recovery and reintegration outcomes.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier varies the quality, rigor, and comprehensiveness of the treatment programs along a single dimension—from poorly designed and ineffective (tier 1) through standard/unspecified (tier 4) to world-leading and maximally effective (tier 7). The core scenario of federal decriminalization with redirected funding remains constant across all tiers.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because higher-quality, more effective addiction treatment programs lead to better recovery outcomes, greater individual well-being, and stronger societal benefits from the decriminalization policy.

original_text: A U.S. federal law requires companies to disclose salary ranges in job postings.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: A U.S. federal law requires companies to disclose only whether a role is hourly or salaried in job postings.

variations.label: low
variations.text: A U.S. federal law requires companies to disclose a broad pay category, such as 'competitive salary,' in job postings.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law requires companies to disclose a general salary range to candidates only upon request during the hiring process.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law requires companies to disclose salary ranges in job postings.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law requires companies to disclose salary ranges along with a summary of benefits and bonus eligibility in job postings.

variations.label: high
variations.text: A U.S. federal law requires companies to disclose full compensation details—including salary ranges, bonus structures, equity grants, and benefits valuations—in job postings.

variations.label: most_preferable

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier increases the depth and richness of disclosure within the same federal-law framework. Tier 1 discloses almost nothing useful; Tier 4 matches the original salary-range requirement; Tiers 5–7 progressively add more meaningful compensation and equity transparency. The distinctions are in scope and depth of information, not in the number of separate laws or unrelated items.

preference_direction: Greater comprehensiveness of disclosure gives job seekers more complete and actionable information about compensation, reducing information asymmetry and enabling fairer negotiations—making higher tiers strictly more preferable.

original_text: A U.S. federal ban on flavored vaping products is implemented to reduce youth usage.
identified_property: effectiveness at reducing youth vaping usage

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: A U.S. federal ban on flavored vaping products is implemented to reduce youth usage, but proves largely ineffective as youth easily circumvent the ban through black-market alternatives, resulting in negligible decline in youth vaping rates.

variations.label: low
variations.text: A U.S. federal ban on flavored vaping products is implemented to reduce youth usage, but achieves only a slight reduction in youth vaping rates as many teens switch to non-flavored products or find other workarounds.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal ban on flavored vaping products is implemented to reduce youth usage, resulting in a modest decline in youth vaping rates, though a notable share of young users continue vaping through alternative means.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal ban on flavored vaping products is implemented to reduce youth usage.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal ban on flavored vaping products is implemented to reduce youth usage, resulting in a significant decline in youth vaping rates as the removal of appealing flavors deters a substantial portion of teen users from continuing.

variations.label: high
variations.text: A U.S. federal ban on flavored vaping products is implemented to reduce youth usage, resulting in a dramatic decline in youth vaping rates as the policy proves highly effective at eliminating the primary driver of teen uptake.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: A U.S. federal ban on flavored vaping products is implemented to reduce youth usage, resulting in a near-total elimination of youth vaping as the policy comprehensively removes the appeal of vaping for minors and is robustly enforced nationwide.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier describes the same core scenario—a U.S. federal ban on flavored vaping products—but varies the effectiveness at reducing youth vaping from negligible impact (tier 1) through modest (tier 3), to moderate/unspecified (tier 4), to significant (tier 5), dramatic (tier 6), and near-total elimination (tier 7). The distinctions are qualitative and each step represents a meaningfully greater degree of effectiveness.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because the stated goal of the policy is to reduce youth vaping usage; greater effectiveness at achieving that goal is strictly better, making near-total elimination of youth vaping the most desirable outcome.

original_text: The U.S. federal government implements a national cap-and-trade system for carbon emissions.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: The U.S. federal government implements a national cap-and-trade system for carbon emissions that is riddled with loopholes, sets an excessively generous cap, lacks enforcement mechanisms, and freely distributes all allowances to incumbent polluters, rendering the system largely ineffective.

variations.label: low
variations.text: The U.S. federal government implements a national cap-and-trade system for carbon emissions with a loosely set cap, limited sector coverage, weak monitoring, and no price floor, resulting in volatile and ineffectively low carbon prices.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. federal government implements a national cap-and-trade system for carbon emissions with a moderately ambitious cap and basic compliance mechanisms, but lacking price stability provisions and offering too many free allowances to meaningfully accelerate emissions reductions.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. federal government implements a national cap-and-trade system for carbon emissions.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. federal government implements a well-designed national cap-and-trade system for carbon emissions featuring broad sector coverage, a steadily declining cap, a price floor to ensure meaningful carbon pricing, and transparent allowance auctions.

variations.label: high
variations.text: The U.S. federal government implements a rigorously designed national cap-and-trade system for carbon emissions with comprehensive sector coverage, science-based declining caps, robust price stability mechanisms, strong enforcement, and auction revenues directed toward clean energy investment and affected communities.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: The U.S. federal government implements an optimally designed national cap-and-trade system for carbon emissions with universal sector coverage, scientifically calibrated declining caps aligned with 1.5°C targets, sophisticated price stability mechanisms, airtight monitoring and enforcement, equitable revenue recycling, and seamless linkage to international carbon markets.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier describes the same core scenario—a U.S. federal cap-and-trade system—but varies the quality and sophistication of its design elements. Tier 1 is a deeply flawed system with loopholes and no enforcement; each subsequent tier adds design rigor, broader coverage, stronger mechanisms, and more thoughtful revenue use, culminating in Tier 7's optimally designed system aligned with the best available climate science and international coordination.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a cap-and-trade system with optimal design—universal coverage, science-aligned caps, price stability, strong enforcement, equitable revenue recycling, and international linkage—maximizes environmental effectiveness, economic efficiency, and social equity, which any rational agent concerned with policy outcomes would prefer.

original_text: Oregon adopts a ranked-choice voting system for state and federal elections.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: Oregon hastily adopts a ranked-choice voting system for only a few local races, with no voter education, outdated tabulation software, and no dedicated funding for proper administration.

variations.label: low
variations.text: Oregon adopts a ranked-choice voting system for select state legislative races, with minimal voter education efforts and only basic upgrades to election infrastructure.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: Oregon adopts a ranked-choice voting system for state elections only, with a modest voter education campaign and standard tabulation infrastructure but no dedicated auditing procedures.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: Oregon adopts a ranked-choice voting system for state and federal elections.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: Oregon adopts a ranked-choice voting system for state and federal elections, accompanied by a well-funded voter education campaign and modernized tabulation infrastructure.

variations.label: high
variations.text: Oregon adopts a ranked-choice voting system for all state and federal elections, with comprehensive voter education, robust auditing procedures, multilingual ballot support, and dedicated long-term funding for administration.

variations.label: most_preferable

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier adds meaningful quality and comprehensiveness dimensions—scope of elections covered, voter education depth, infrastructure modernization, auditing rigor, accessibility, oversight, and stakeholder engagement—while preserving the core scenario of Oregon adopting ranked-choice voting.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a ranked-choice voting system implemented with maximum quality—extensive education, modern infrastructure, independent oversight, accessibility, and iterative improvement—is far more likely to function fairly, earn public trust, and achieve the democratic goals motivating its adoption.

original_text: The U.S. federal government guarantees free public college tuition for all students.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: The U.S. federal government offers a modest tuition discount at public community colleges for low-income students who meet strict eligibility requirements.

variations.label: low
variations.text: The U.S. federal government guarantees free tuition at public community colleges for all students, with no coverage of fees or other educational costs.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. federal government guarantees free tuition at public two-year and four-year colleges for all students, though mandatory fees and ancillary costs remain uncovered.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. federal government guarantees free public college tuition for all students.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. federal government guarantees free public college tuition, mandatory fees, and textbooks for all students.

variations.label: high
variations.text: The U.S. federal government guarantees free tuition, fees, textbooks, and room and board at all public colleges and universities for all students.

variations.label: most_preferable

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier expands the comprehensiveness and quality of educational coverage along clear dimensions: scope of eligible institutions (community college → all accredited institutions), breadth of costs covered (partial tuition discount → tuition, fees, textbooks, housing, stipend, and support services), and eligibility (restricted low-income → universal). The core scenario—federal government support for college education—is preserved throughout.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because it offers the most comprehensive and highest-quality coverage: universal eligibility, inclusion of both public and private institutions, full coverage of all educational and living expenses, and added academic and career support—maximizing the benefit to students.

original_text: Net neutrality protections are reinstated in the United States.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: Voluntary, non-binding net neutrality guidelines are issued in the United States, with no enforcement mechanism and broad exemptions for ISPs.

variations.label: low
variations.text: Limited net neutrality transparency rules are adopted in the United States, requiring ISPs to disclose throttling practices but not prohibiting them.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: Partial net neutrality protections are reinstated in the United States, prohibiting outright blocking but still permitting paid prioritization and some forms of throttling.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: Net neutrality protections are reinstated in the United States.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: Robust net neutrality protections are reinstated in the United States, with clear bans on blocking, throttling, and paid prioritization, backed by a well-funded enforcement body.

variations.label: high
variations.text: Comprehensive net neutrality protections are enacted into permanent federal law in the United States, covering both fixed and mobile broadband, with strong enforcement powers, meaningful penalties for violations, and explicit prohibitions on zero-rating schemes.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: Comprehensive net neutrality protections are permanently enshrined in federal law in the United States, covering all broadband technologies, with stringent enforcement, substantial penalties, bans on zero-rating and interconnection abuses, regular public audits of ISP practices, and provisions ensuring protections cannot be weakened by future regulatory changes.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier represents a meaningfully stronger and more comprehensive form of net neutrality protection. Tier 1 offers only voluntary guidelines with no teeth; tier 2 adds disclosure requirements but no prohibitions; tier 3 prohibits blocking but allows paid prioritization; tier 4 is the standard reinstatement; tier 5 adds robust enforcement; tier 6 codifies protections into permanent law with expanded scope including zero-rating; tier 7 adds maximum durability, auditing, interconnection protections, and safeguards against future rollback. The core scenario—net neutrality policy action in the U.S.—is preserved throughout.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because it represents the strongest, most durable, and most comprehensive net neutrality framework, ensuring maximum protection of open internet principles with robust enforcement, broad scope, and resilience against future weakening.

original_text: A U.S. federal law prohibits gender-affirming care for minors nationwide.
identified_property: scope and strictness of the prohibition

variations.label: least_preferable

variations.label: low
variations.text: A U.S. federal law criminalizes all gender-affirming medical and psychological care for minors nationwide, imposing criminal penalties on healthcare providers and revoking medical licenses, with no exceptions for ongoing treatments.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law prohibits all gender-affirming medical care for minors nationwide, including puberty blockers, hormones, and surgeries, with no exceptions even for those already undergoing treatment.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law prohibits gender-affirming care for minors nationwide.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: A U.S. federal law prohibits gender-affirming surgical procedures and cross-sex hormones for minors nationwide, but permits puberty blockers and mental health counseling.

variations.label: high
variations.text: A U.S. federal law restricts gender-affirming surgical procedures for minors under 16 nationwide, while permitting hormonal treatments, puberty blockers, and counseling with parental and physician consent.

variations.label: most_preferable

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier progressively narrows the scope and reduces the strictness of the restriction. Tier 1 expands the age range to 25, criminalizes even counseling, and penalizes facilitators. Tier 7 merely adds a consent/review process for surgeries only. The core scenario—federal legislation regulating minors' access to gender-affirming care—is preserved throughout.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because it imposes only a minimal procedural requirement (consent and review) on the narrowest category of care (surgery only), preserving the broadest access to gender-affirming care for minors while maintaining reasonable safeguards.

original_text: The United States re-enters the Paris Climate Agreement and commits to net-zero emissions by 2050.
identified_property: strength and credibility of the commitment

variations.label: least_preferable

variations.label: low
variations.text: The United States re-enters the Paris Climate Agreement and issues a symbolic pledge to pursue emissions reductions by 2050, but without concrete targets, a formal implementation plan, or dedicated funding.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: The United States re-enters the Paris Climate Agreement and sets an aspirational goal of net-zero emissions by 2050, but without enforceable interim milestones or dedicated federal funding to support the transition.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: The United States re-enters the Paris Climate Agreement and commits to net-zero emissions by 2050.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: The United States re-enters the Paris Climate Agreement, commits to net-zero emissions by 2050, and backs the commitment with binding interim reduction targets and substantial dedicated federal funding.

variations.label: high
variations.text: The United States re-enters the Paris Climate Agreement, commits to net-zero emissions by 2050 through bipartisan enacted legislation, with binding interim targets, robust dedicated funding, and independent progress monitoring with public reporting.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: The United States re-enters the Paris Climate Agreement and enacts comprehensive, durable legislation mandating net-zero emissions by 2050, with legally enforceable interim targets, fully appropriated federal funding, independent third-party verification, and automatic corrective measures triggered by any shortfall in progress.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier increases the strength and credibility of the US climate commitment along qualitative dimensions: from a vague, non-binding aspiration (tier 1) through symbolic pledges and aspirational goals, up to the original commitment (tier 4), and then progressively adding binding interim targets, dedicated funding, legislative codification, independent verification, and automatic enforcement mechanisms (tiers 5–7). The core scenario—the US re-entering the Paris Agreement and pursuing net-zero—is preserved throughout.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a commitment backed by comprehensive legislation, enforceable interim milestones, full funding, independent verification, and automatic corrective mechanisms is far more credible and likely to achieve actual net-zero emissions than a weaker or merely aspirational pledge.

variations.text: A U.S. federal law increases educational funding nationwide.
